fr:mainmatter.html:p: In C++, you allocate memory on the heap with new and delete.

Concept-level notes on C++, working from the ground up: the kinds of types you can define, how concrete and abstract types differ in their memory model, the machinery behind runtime polymorphism, and the language features that govern immutability, type deduction, error handling, and storage. Each section transcludes an atomic tree; the notebook is the organizing spine, so links to "C++" anywhere in the forest resolve here.

C++ gives several ways to bundle data into a named type. A class is the workhorse — it separates interface from implementation through access control, and carries the constructor/destructor machinery that ties resource lifetime to scope. A struct is the same mechanism with public defaults, a union overlays members in shared storage, and an enumeration names a fixed set of values.
Need an abstraction to separate implementation from interface. This is done in C++ via the public and private members of a class.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A struct is class with its members public by default.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A constructor is a function of a class that shares the same name as the class.
A constructor without parameters is called a default constructor.
A destructor is the mechanism in C++ that ensures that any memory a constructor allocates is deallocated.
A destructor is a function of a class that shares the same name as the class with the ~ operator to denote it is the complement of the constructor.

A struct is a class whose members are public by default. The two are the same mechanism — struct simply flips the default access — so the choice is a convention: use struct for passive aggregates of data with no invariant to maintain, and class when access control is enforcing an invariant.
A union is a struct in which all members are allocated at the same address. This ensures that the union occupies only as much space as its largest member.
A union can hold a value for only one member at a time.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. No automatic lifetime management for non-trivial types. If a union holds an object with a non-trivial constructor/destructor (like std::string), the compiler won't call them for you. You must manually placement-new and explicitly call the destructor, and getting this wrong causes leaks or corruption. union U { std::string s; int i; }; // destructor not called automatically

fr:mainmatter.html:p: User-defined type in C++ that is a set of named values designed to make code more readable and less error prone.

html:div.html:pre:
enum class Color { red, green, blue };
Color c = Color::red;

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If you want enumerator values to be int, you can use enum.
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Example. enum Color { red, green, blue };
int color = green // color = 1 Note that this is an error if Color is enum class.
Remark. Use enumerations to represent sets of named constants
Remark. Prefer enum class to enum when possible

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The pivotal design axis is whether a type's representation is part of its definition. A concrete class commits to its layout, so it can live on the stack and be copied by value; an abstract type hides representation entirely behind pure virtual functions, which forces heap allocation and pointer-based use. The container is the running example that both build on.
A class that behaves "just like built-in types". The defining characteristic of a concrete type is that its representation is part of its definition.
Because the representation is fixed you can

html:ol.html:li: Allocate objects directly on the stack.
Copy and assign them by value
Have them as direct members of other classes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. complex, vector, and string are concrete classes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A container is a concrete class that exists to hold elements..
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. We call vector a container.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: We can initialize a container via std::initializer_list, where you directly pass the list of elements via {1,2,3,4,...}. Alternatively, containers may support additional methods like push_back().

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An abstract type is a type that completely insulates a user from implementation details.
Since we do not know anything about the representation (in particular, the size) we must allocate everything on heap.

Abstract types are only useful because of dynamic dispatch. A virtual function call is resolved through a hidden per-object pointer into a per-class virtual function table, which also carries the type information that makes dynamic casting possible.
Each class with a virtual function gets a virtual table: a static, per-class, array of function pointers with one entry per virtual function.
The virtual table is created by the compiler and is stored in the object's memory.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: vtable layout: [ offset-to-top ] // slot -2 [ type_info* ] // slot -1 ← RTTI [ &virtual_fn_0 ] // slot 0 [ &virtual_fn_1 ] // slot 1 ...
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The type_info pointer is needed to support runtime type-checking / dynamic casting .
fr:mainmatter.html:p: During construction, the vptr is initialized to point to the base class's vtable. In each classes vtable, there is a thunk (offset-to-top) that tells

html:div.html:pre: offset 0: [ vptr_A ] ← D's primary vtable (A subobject) offset 8: [ a ] offset 16: [ vptr_B ] ← D's secondary vtable (B subobject) offset 24: [ b ] offset 32: [ d ]

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every object of a class with a virtual function carries a hidden vptr (virtual pointer) that points to the class's virtual table.

Three keywords that shift guarantees and work between the programmer, the reader, and the compiler. const is a promise not to mutate, constexpr pushes that promise further into compile-time evaluation, and auto hands type deduction to the compiler — indispensable for generic programming.
A hint to the compiler in C++, meaning "I promise not to change this value".

A hint to the compiler in C++ that the value or function can be evaluated at compile time. This allows the compiler to pre-compute the function / value and hardcode it into the resulting binary, eliminating any instructions involved in the computation from the final binary.

html:p.html:em: Idea. Shift work from every run on every user's machine to once on the build machine.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A constexpr function is not forced to run at compile time. It is only done only when the arguments are const.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: auto is automatic type deduction in C++.
In Generic Programming, auto is crucial because types may not be known.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. When should you NOT use auto?

Answer. In C++ they provide two reasons:
When you want to make the type visible clearly to the readers of your code.
You want to be explicit on the variable range or precision i.e. auto x = 1; will be an int. If you want to enforce a double, then you must specify so explicitly.
Remark. auto x{v}; deduces single value's type in modern C++, but in C++11/14 it deduces std::initializer_list. auto x = {v}; remains an std::initializer_list in modern C++.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In C++, exceptions are thrown via the throw keyword.
The throw keyword transfers control to exception handler. This then proceeds as

html:ol.html:li: Allocate heap memory for the exception object.
Walk the stack frame-by-frame to checking to see if any frame has a catch with matching type.
Walks the stack again, restoring registers / SP per frame.

html:ul.html:li: Cleaning up all the invalid stack frames between the throw and catch.

html:ol.html:li: Transfers control to the catch block.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If no matching catch block is found, the program calls std::terminate
You can annotate a function with noexcept to indicate that it never throws exceptions. This is mainly used as an annotation to enable better choices by the compiler
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. The compiler may call copy constructor instead of move constructor if the move constructer is not marked noexcept; If an exception were thrown during moving, then the object would be in a broken state.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In C++, you allocate memory on the heap with new and delete.

The chunk of memory the OS gives a process to use at its own discretion. Each process has its own address space, so that one process cannot read or overwrite another's program state. An address space typically contains three regions:

html:ol.html:li: Stack: local variables, function arguments, return values.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every address a process sees is virtual — not the location in physical memory. The hardware translates virtual addresses to physical ones at access time. Approaches to that translation include Base and bounds, Segmentation, and Paging.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The API the user sees is tiny: a way to get memory and a way to release it, exposed as malloc and free. The common bugs are forgetting to initialize, forgetting to free, freeing too early (dangling pointer), freeing twice (double free), and freeing memory not from malloc (invalid free).
Remark. malloc and free are library calls, not syscalls. The heap is pre-allocated; malloc extends it by invoking brk/sbrk when it runs out.
Remark. When a process dies, the OS reclaims its memory, so a bounded leak is not catastrophic — just bad practice.
Remark. You can also acquire an anonymous heap-like region directly via mmap.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I often hear this question come up amongst performance-driven developers, and for the longest time I could never get a straight answer as to why the stack was so much better? After a long foray into the inner workings of how memory allocation is actually done, I think I finally have an answer. I do not want to forget this, so I am doing a quick write up here.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
This all comes down to what happens during each memory allocation. Prior to actually running the program, the operating system allocates some amount of memory for both the stack and the heap (I think around 2-4MB?). When you allocate memory on the stack i.e. int x = 1;
Stack just increments stack pointer.
Writes value to new address pointed to by stack pointer. When you allocate memory on the heap i.e. int *x = malloc(sizeof(int)), we must instead choose how best to fulfill the request, which is a whole research direction in itself.
One such implementation is the buddy algorithm However, malloc itself is not a system call and therefore is not terribly slow. A real issue occurs when we run out of pre-allocated heap memory. This requires invoking a system call like sbrk to increment the size of the allocated memory for the heap.
If no available pages, OS must evict and write to disk...
Formally, this is known as "heap growth". Generally, if you know how much memory you need it is good practice to just allocated all up front to minimize any potential system calls. With this, the performance difference is not too drastic :)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Concept-level notes on C++, working from the ground up: the kinds of types you can define, how concrete and abstract types differ in their memory model, the machinery behind runtime polymorphism, and the language features that govern immutability, type deduction, error handling, and storage. Each section transcludes an atomic tree; the notebook is the organizing spine, so links to "C++" anywhere in the forest resolve here.

C++ gives several ways to bundle data into a named type. A class is the workhorse — it separates interface from implementation through access control, and carries the constructor/destructor machinery that ties resource lifetime to scope. A struct is the same mechanism with public defaults, a union overlays members in shared storage, and an enumeration names a fixed set of values.
Need an abstraction to separate implementation from interface. This is done in C++ via the public and private members of a class.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A struct is class with its members public by default.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A constructor is a function of a class that shares the same name as the class.
A constructor without parameters is called a default constructor.
A destructor is the mechanism in C++ that ensures that any memory a constructor allocates is deallocated.
A destructor is a function of a class that shares the same name as the class with the ~ operator to denote it is the complement of the constructor.

A struct is a class whose members are public by default. The two are the same mechanism — struct simply flips the default access — so the choice is a convention: use struct for passive aggregates of data with no invariant to maintain, and class when access control is enforcing an invariant.
A union is a struct in which all members are allocated at the same address. This ensures that the union occupies only as much space as its largest member.
A union can hold a value for only one member at a time.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. No automatic lifetime management for non-trivial types. If a union holds an object with a non-trivial constructor/destructor (like std::string), the compiler won't call them for you. You must manually placement-new and explicitly call the destructor, and getting this wrong causes leaks or corruption. union U { std::string s; int i; }; // destructor not called automatically

fr:mainmatter.html:p: User-defined type in C++ that is a set of named values designed to make code more readable and less error prone.

html:div.html:pre:
enum class Color { red, green, blue };
Color c = Color::red;

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If you want enumerator values to be int, you can use enum.
fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Example. enum Color { red, green, blue };
int color = green // color = 1 Note that this is an error if Color is enum class.
Remark. Use enumerations to represent sets of named constants
Remark. Prefer enum class to enum when possible

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The pivotal design axis is whether a type's representation is part of its definition. A concrete class commits to its layout, so it can live on the stack and be copied by value; an abstract type hides representation entirely behind pure virtual functions, which forces heap allocation and pointer-based use. The container is the running example that both build on.
A class that behaves "just like built-in types". The defining characteristic of a concrete type is that its representation is part of its definition.
Because the representation is fixed you can

html:ol.html:li: Allocate objects directly on the stack.
Copy and assign them by value
Have them as direct members of other classes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. complex, vector, and string are concrete classes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A container is a concrete class that exists to hold elements..
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. We call vector a container.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: We can initialize a container via std::initializer_list, where you directly pass the list of elements via {1,2,3,4,...}. Alternatively, containers may support additional methods like push_back().

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An abstract type is a type that completely insulates a user from implementation details.
Since we do not know anything about the representation (in particular, the size) we must allocate everything on heap.

Abstract types are only useful because of dynamic dispatch. A virtual function call is resolved through a hidden per-object pointer into a per-class virtual function table, which also carries the type information that makes dynamic casting possible.
Each class with a virtual function gets a virtual table: a static, per-class, array of function pointers with one entry per virtual function.
The virtual table is created by the compiler and is stored in the object's memory.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: vtable layout: [ offset-to-top ] // slot -2 [ type_info* ] // slot -1 ← RTTI [ &virtual_fn_0 ] // slot 0 [ &virtual_fn_1 ] // slot 1 ...
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The type_info pointer is needed to support runtime type-checking / dynamic casting .
fr:mainmatter.html:p: During construction, the vptr is initialized to point to the base class's vtable. In each classes vtable, there is a thunk (offset-to-top) that tells

html:div.html:pre: offset 0: [ vptr_A ] ← D's primary vtable (A subobject) offset 8: [ a ] offset 16: [ vptr_B ] ← D's secondary vtable (B subobject) offset 24: [ b ] offset 32: [ d ]

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Every object of a class with a virtual function carries a hidden vptr (virtual pointer) that points to the class's virtual table.

Three keywords that shift guarantees and work between the programmer, the reader, and the compiler. const is a promise not to mutate, constexpr pushes that promise further into compile-time evaluation, and auto hands type deduction to the compiler — indispensable for generic programming.
A hint to the compiler in C++, meaning "I promise not to change this value".

A hint to the compiler in C++ that the value or function can be evaluated at compile time. This allows the compiler to pre-compute the function / value and hardcode it into the resulting binary, eliminating any instructions involved in the computation from the final binary.

html:p.html:em: Idea. Shift work from every run on every user's machine to once on the build machine.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. A constexpr function is not forced to run at compile time. It is only done only when the arguments are const.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: auto is automatic type deduction in C++.
In Generic Programming, auto is crucial because types may not be known.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. When should you NOT use auto?

Answer. In C++ they provide two reasons:
When you want to make the type visible clearly to the readers of your code.
You want to be explicit on the variable range or precision i.e. auto x = 1; will be an int. If you want to enforce a double, then you must specify so explicitly.
Remark. auto x{v}; deduces single value's type in modern C++, but in C++11/14 it deduces std::initializer_list. auto x = {v}; remains an std::initializer_list in modern C++.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In C++, exceptions are thrown via the throw keyword.
The throw keyword transfers control to exception handler. This then proceeds as

html:ol.html:li: Allocate heap memory for the exception object.
Walk the stack frame-by-frame to checking to see if any frame has a catch with matching type.
Walks the stack again, restoring registers / SP per frame.

html:ul.html:li: Cleaning up all the invalid stack frames between the throw and catch.

html:ol.html:li: Transfers control to the catch block.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If no matching catch block is found, the program calls std::terminate
You can annotate a function with noexcept to indicate that it never throws exceptions. This is mainly used as an annotation to enable better choices by the compiler
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. The compiler may call copy constructor instead of move constructor if the move constructer is not marked noexcept; If an exception were thrown during moving, then the object would be in a broken state.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In C++, you allocate memory on the heap with new and delete.
